reasoning: proxy_requests() is registered directly on the 'template_redirect' action with no authentication requirement, no nonce, no capability check, and no per-request secret. The only two guards are: (1) Portal_Options::get_proxy_mappings_enabled() must return true — this is a site-wide plugin configuration toggle, not a per-request secret; and (2) the incoming request URI must match one of six predefined HubSpot path patterns (/_hcms/, /hs/, /hubfs/, /hs-fs/, /cs/c/, /e3t/) or a cached proxy mapping stored in the 'proxy_mappings' transient. Both conditions are infrastructure-level, not attacker-controlled secrets. Any unauthenticated visitor who navigates to a matching URL (e.g. https://example.com/_hcms/anything) on a site where proxy mappings are enabled will trigger the full proxy execution path. get_request_headers() collects all HTTP request headers including Cookie, and filter_wordpress_cookies() only strips three prefixes: 'wordpress_', 'wp-settings-', and 'wp_woocommerce_'. Cookies from WooCommerce sessions (wc_session_cookie_<hash>), PHP sessions (PHPSESSID), security plugins (wordfence_verifiedHuman, sucuri_cloudproxy, etc.), and any other non-WordPress-prefixed cookie are forwarded unfiltered to the external HubSpot CDN. The gate is fully breachable by any unauthenticated visitor. Original severity of medium is maintained because the recipient is HubSpot (a known third party, not an arbitrary attacker-controlled server), but the data leakage to a third-party CDN without user consent is real and exploitable without any credential.
